metadata.title: Fig. 5. Comparisons of Jiangsu's annual emissions from different studies. The range line represents the  95%  CIs around the central estimates of the provincial inventory by this work.
references: Fig. 5 compares the central estimates of Jiangsu's emissions (at provincial level) by this work with those of other studies. As limited information was available for the same year as this work (2012), the results for 2010 and 2014 were included for comparisons as well. Those inventories were developed either at national scale (MEIC; Xia et al., 2016) or regional scale (Fu et al., 2013; Shanghai Research Academy of Environmental Science, SRAES). It can be seen that most estimates are within the  $95\%$  CIs calculated in the current study for gaseous pollutants, BC and OC. Inter-annual variability could partly explain the lower estimates by SRAES and Fu et al. (2013). Different data sources (in particular the penetrations and removal rates of emission control technologies) in multiple-scale inventories also contributed to the discrepancies in emission estimates. For  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , emissions from other studies were much smaller than this work at provincial level, even out of the  $95\%$  CIs around the central estimates. This large discrepancy came mainly from the difference in the removal rates of dust collectors used in various inventories. While all other studies applied the national/regional averages of dust removal rates based on limited test samples to calculate the emissions, the plant-specific data obtained from on-site investigations generally implied much lower removal rates for the province, leading to larger emission estimates in the provincial inventory. The comparison revealed the importance of developing and applying local emission factors on establishment of high-resolution emission inventory whenever possible.

Fig. 4. The distributions of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (a) and  $\mathrm{NO_X}$  (b) emissions from power plants in Jiangsu in 2012, using Monte-Carlo simulation. The red and black bars represent the results from the national and provincial inventories, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)  
Fig. 5. Comparisons of Jiangsu's annual emissions from different studies. The range line represents the  $95\%$  CIs around the central estimates of the provincial inventory by this work.

Following our previous work (Zhao et al., 2011), Monte Carlo simulation was applied to quantify the uncertainties of national inventory by source category for the whole country and Jiangsu province. In the simulation framework, uncertainty of each parameter was characterized by probability distribution function (PDF), and the framework randomly generated values for uncertain variables over and over to simulate a model. A simulation calculated numerous scenarios of a model by repeatedly picking values from the PDFs for all the uncertain variables, and the uncertainty of target (emissions in this case) was determined according to the ranges of calculated scenarios. The PDF was fitted for parameters with adequate measurement data, using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (K-A test) for the goodness-of-fit  $(\mathrm{p} = 0.05)$ . For parameters with limited measurements, and those that fail to pass the goodness-of-fit test, probability distributions must be assumed. In particular, the uncertainties of activity levels for power sector were taken from Zhao et al. (2011). Small difference was found between the annual coal consumption officially reported for the entire country and that from the sum of provincial consumption of power plants, implying good reliability of statistics for the sector (Zhao et al., 2013). Following the guideline by IPCC (2006), normal distribution was assumed and the coefficients of variation (CV, the standard deviation divided by the mean) was set at  $5\%$  for activity data of power sector. For other industrial sources, enhanced uncertainty of coal consumption was found through comparisons between the old and updated national energy statistics, and it would lead to an apparent uncertainty of  $38\%$  in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions at most (Hong et al., 2017). Accordingly, CV of activity data for industrial sources other than power sector was elevated to  $20\%$  in this work (i.e., the  $95\%$  confidence interval (CI) was  $39.2\%$  around the central estimate). Determination of PDFs for emission factors will be discussed in details in the following section. All of the input

on uncertainties of Nanjing emissions by sector. As shown in Table 5, emissions and their uncertainties of industrial sector were taken from current work, and emissions from residential and transportation sectors were kept unchanged from Zhao et al. (2015). As little progress was made to improve the uncertainty analysis of city-scale emissions from the latter two sectors, the relative ranges around the central estimates for national inventory were applied (Zhao et al., 2011). For fugitive dust, lognormal distributions with a CV at  $100\%$  was tentatively assumed to reflect the highly uncertain estimates. With 10,000 Monte-Carlo simulations, the  $95\%$  CIs of BC to CO and OC to BC emission ratios were calculated at 0.0033–0.0133 and 0.79–2.92, with central estimates at 0.0074 and 1.39, respectively. In contrast, the "top-down" constraints from ground observation were 0.0084 and 1.59, respectively, i.e., they were  $14\%$  larger than the bottom-up ratios, and within the  $95\%$  CIs. It should be noted that uncertainty also existed in the method of "top-down" constraint estimation. For example, the uncertainty of BC to CO emission ratio derived from observation was estimated at  $20\%$ , as the wet, dry deposition and chemistry of the species could not be fully understood (Wang et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2015). Moreover, the primary OC to BC emission ratio was estimated with a semi-quantitative method, EC-tracer method. The determination of the ratio was arbitrary and unable to obtain single OC to BC ratio that represented a mixture of primary sources varying in time and space. Errors could be caused by the occasional irregular contributions from sources with a primary OC to EC ratio vastly different from the usual mix of sources (Cui et al., 2015). Therefore, the comparison between the bottom-up and top-down estimates needed to be interpreted with caution. Judged by the contribution to the variance of emission ratio, industrial CO and BC emissions were identified to be most significant to the uncertainty of primary BC to CO ratio, with the contribution to variance calculated at  $36\%$  and  $25\%$ , respectively. Industrial OC, fugitive OC, and industrial BC emissions were identified to be most significant to uncertainty of primary OC to BC, with the contribution to variance at  $25\%$ ,  $23\%$ , and  $20\%$ , respectively. The result highlighted the importance of industrial emissions on the "bottom-up" emission ratio estimates.

content.title: Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).

metadata.source_md: How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md
metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\figure_1.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).
references: After seven-year implementing of Air Clean Plan, the mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations clearly decreased  $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ $33.65\%)$  in China. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the first phase (2014-2017) were 35.97  $\pm 15.11~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ , which decreased to  $28.92\pm 12.76~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$  in the latter phase (2018-2020) of the China ACP. In both phases, high concentrations were observed mainly in northwest China, northern China, and a small proportion of central China (i.e., Henan and Hubei provinces) (Fig. 1). The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased in over  $68.09\%$  of the area in mainland China during the period of APPCAP (Fig. 1b). A clear reduction was detected in BTH (decreased by  $17.33~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $25.81\%$ ), followed by YRD (decreased by  $12.79~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $24.07\%$ ) and parts of central China (decreased by  $14.66~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $22.93\%$ ) during the APPCAP

Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\mu \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the "Beautiful China" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\sim 98\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.

During the period of APPCAP, the control policies essentially contributed to the decrease of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 8a). The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  reduction was sourced primarily from the clean production policy (net decrease contribution of  $10.53\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the energy consumption control measure (net decrease contribution of  $7.28\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and fuel structure optimization (net decrease contribution of  $3.08\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ). Economic growth was the primary driver for increasing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution from 2014 to 2017, with a net contribution of  $9.00\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The co-effect of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in this period also intensified  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. During the APWBSD, the contribution of clean production, energy consumption control and fuel structure optimization to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decrease were  $85\%$ ,  $37\%$  and  $21\%$ , respectively. Compared with the period of ACPCAP, the effectiveness of all measures decreases in APWBSD, especially the clean production measures (from  $-257\%$  to  $-85\%$  ) and

We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

Our analysis of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  attributed deaths in China was compared with several recent studies (Table S3). Great progresses was made in previous studies (Zhang et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019; McDuffie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019; Apte et al., 2015), albeit with several limitations in common: (1) previous studies focused mainly on the APPCAP (2013-2017) and lack the updated results of the APWBSD (2018-2020); (2) the chemical transport model was used widely in previous research, which showed advantages for predicting the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without ground measurement, but also has limitations in terms of prediction accuracy (Ma et al., 2022); (3) the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model used for premature death estimation in previous studies lacked sufficient information on deaths at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and failed to separate exposure from ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and secondhand smoke, which could potentially introduce uncertainties (Burnett et al., 2014). Our research provides several advances compared to previous studies. First, we quantified the co-benefit of the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measures in China for the environment and health from 2014 to 2020. Second, we used state-of-the-art  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products with the highest accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 for detail) and the finest resolution (spatial resolution of  $1\mathrm{km}$ , Fig. S2) at the national scale to offer a full-coverage estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. With these innovations, we not only provided a more accurate estimation of the risk due to exposure to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , but also to analyze the great spatially heterogeneity of policy effectiveness. Third, we adopted the updated MR-BRT spline method, which fits the risk with a more flexible shape for more reliable estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths (Hystad et al., 2020). The MR-BRT includes more exposure scenarios at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels during fitting compared with other models such as the IER model (Yusuf et al., 2020; Chowdhury et al., 2022). It also excludes active smoking to control for and reduce potential bias due to this effect (Pope et al., 2018). Finally, our study estimated the co-benefit from  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to the environment and health under the background of the ACP and NDC pledges for the first time.

metadata.title: Fig. 6. An overview of  PM_{2.5} -attributable disease burden conditions. (a) the proportion of six diseases in total deaths, and (b) the number of deaths in different ages (stroke: cerebrovascular disease; DM: type II diabetes; IHD: ischemic heart disease; LC: lung cancer; LRI: lower respiratory infections; COPD: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease).
references: The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths were primarily caused by stroke (cerebrovascular disease) and lung cancer (LC) in China, accounting for  $41.12\%$  and  $30.55\%$  of the total number of premature deaths, respectively (Fig. 6a). This was followed by chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and ischemic heart disease (IHD), which accounted for  $14.4\%$  and  $9.6\%$  of premature deaths, accordingly. The proportion of deaths from each disease was fairly stable between 2014 and 2020. The proportion of deaths due to ischemic heart disease (IHD), lung cancer (LC) and lower respiratory infections (LRI) increased slightly by  $1.41\%$ ,  $2.81\%$ , and  $0.58\%$ , while the proportion of deaths caused by stroke, DM and COPD decreased slightly by  $2.35\%$ ,  $0.27\%$  and  $2.19\%$ .

metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_4.jpg
metadata.title: Fig.4 Number of industrial heat source objects and industrial heat anomaly points in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas
metadata.supplement: (a) 工业热源对象 (b) 工业热源热异常点 图4 京津冀及周边地区工业热源对象和工业热异常点数量
references: Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The

concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.
Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas

metadata.title: Fig. 5. Trend of annual average  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018. Observed  PM_{2.5}  concentrations were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
references: Guided by the Action Plan, many other cities in China are following Beijing to reduce ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 5). The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\%$  for BTH,  $34\%$  for YRD, and  $28\%$  for PRD. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\mu \mathrm{g}\cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . This progress has motivated some local governments to actively target more stringent limits on  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$

(b)  
Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  $8\mathrm{hO}_3$  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.

metadata.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
references: The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.

from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.

$\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.

Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. In order to find out the content of crustal elements in the

The mass concentration of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.
Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.

In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\%$  and  $63.36\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\%$  and  $93.32\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.

The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the

The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{F}^{-} > \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\%$ ,  $1.788\%$ ,  $0.773\%$ ,  $2.186\%$  and  $0.623\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\%$ ,  $0.789\%$ ,  $1.782\%$ ,  $2.918\%$  and  $0.857\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. Thus, humidity helped increase the percentage of water-soluble ions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and still keep the order of top water-soluble ions. Cheng et al. (2010) studied the on-road  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Hong Kong, a coastal city with higher humidity than Wuhan. They found the percentage of

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.

It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .

The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by

The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.

Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.

content.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: Figure 6 illustrates the emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles under China III, IV, and V standards.
content.insight: The figure shows higher emission factors for Na, Mg, K, and Ca, indicating significant contributions from fuel additives, with lower emissions for other elements from engine wear and catalytic particles.

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.
# 3.1. Emission factors and rates of  $PM_{2,5}$
# 3.4. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.

It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .

The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.

The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . Their percentages in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased slightly with high humidity and the order of top water-soluble ions kept the same. Their emission factors of diesel vehicles were all larger than gasoline vehicles. The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. It occupied  $53.93\%$  and  $51.15\%$  in gasoline and diesel vehicles, respectively. Secondly, EC occupied  $13.07\%$  in gasoline vehicles while  $25.17\%$  in diesel vehicles.

content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
references: Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\mathrm{h}$ $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\%$  and  $24.5\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\% -29\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.

The biggest challenge of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy.

content.title: Fig. 3. The average emission rate of  PM_2.5  in different speed segments.
content.summary: The figure depicts the average emission rates of PM_2.5 for gasoline and diesel vehicles across different speed segments.
content.insight: Higher driving speeds tend to increase PM_2.5 emission rates for both gasoline and diesel vehicles, indicating a positive correlation between speed and emissions.

metadata.source_md: On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md
metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_3.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 3. The average emission rate of  PM_2.5  in different speed segments.
metadata.supplement: average emission rate(mg/s) range of driving speed(km/h)
references: The mass concentration of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

A portable vehicle on-board test system was used in this experiment to detect the vehicle emissions and collected  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  samples, and the schematic diagram of PEMS was shown in Fig. s4. The test route included the urban expressway, the main road, and the secondary main road and branch road. The driving route of the motor vehicle test was shown in Fig. s5 in the supporting information. There is no traffic light in the expressway of the third ring road, and the average speed exceeds 60 km/h. There are 10 traffic lights in the main roads, where the traffic is heavy and the traffic jams often happen. The average speed of the tested vehicle is less than  $20\mathrm{km / h}$  when traveling on the main roads. Motor vehicles travel in different speeds under different road conditions, and present different  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates.

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of 6 gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 3a.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $1\#$  increased gradually with the rising speed when the driving speed was below  $60~\mathrm{km / h}$ . It reached a peak emission rate of  $0.0491~\mathrm{mg / s}$  in the speed range of  $50 - 60~\mathrm{km / h}$ , and then turned to decrease as the driving speed exceeded  $60~\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of vehicles  $2\#$ ,  $3\#$  and  $5\#$  showed the similar changing trend. They increased firstly with the rising speed when the driving speed was below  $50~\mathrm{km / h}$ . The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates showed a slight decrease in the speed range of  $50 - 60~\mathrm{km / h}$ , and then continued to rise up to their maximum after the driving speed exceeded  $60~\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of vehicles  $4\#$  and  $6\#$  were different from those of the other 4 gasoline vehicles. No obvious changes of their  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates were observed with the increasing driving speed. For gasoline vehicles, the average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate was  $0.0054~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed  $(V \leq 20~\mathrm{km / h})$ . It turned to  $0.0161~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < V \leq 60~\mathrm{km / h})$ , and increased to  $0.0216~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under high speed  $(V > 60~\mathrm{km / h})$ . Generally, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of gasoline vehicles showed a rising trend as the driving speed increased. The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of 4 diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 3b.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $7\#$  increased gradually with the rising speed, and it reached peak emission rate of  $0.4814~\mathrm{mg / s}$  in the speed range of above  $60~\mathrm{km / h}$ .  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of Vehicles  $8\#$  and  $10\#$  showed the similar changing trend. They increased gradually with the rising speed, and reached their maximum in the speed range of above  $60~\mathrm{km / h}$ . The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of Vehicle  $9\#$  showed a fluctuating rise with increasing speed. For diesel vehicles, the average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate was  $0.0879~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed  $(V \leq 20~\mathrm{km / h})$ . It turned to  $0.1696~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < V \leq 60~\mathrm{km / h})$ , and increased to  $0.2084~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under high speed  $(V > 60~\mathrm{km / h})$ . Generally, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of diesel

The average emission rate of the tested vehicles under low speed  $(\mathrm{V} \leq 20 \mathrm{~km/h})$  was  $0.0384 \mathrm{mg/s}$ , it increased to  $0.0775 \mathrm{mg/s}$  under medium speed  $(20 < \mathrm{V} \leq 60 \mathrm{~km/h})$ , and finally reached up to  $0.0964 \mathrm{mg/s}$  under high speed  $(\mathrm{V} > 60 \mathrm{~km/h})$ . Therefore, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of vehicles in Wuhan was positively correlated with the driving speed of vehicles. That was, an increase in driving speed would increase the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rate of vehicles to a certain extent.

metadata.title: Fig. 8. Correlation coefficients between air quality change and socioeconomic factors.
references: The Kendall correlation coefficients depicted the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, as shown in Fig. 8. Ordinary least squares analysis was also done to assess the variance inflation factors, which ranged from 1.007 to 2.297 and were all less than the threshold of 10, indicating that there was no multiple collinearity issue and no high correlation among socioeconomic variables, which made the subsequent correlation analysis more reliable.

As depicted in Fig. 8, the socioeconomic factors were seen not to be highly associated with the change rate of AQI. Amongst, an obvious observation was that the increase in environmental governance and a decrease in industrialization were positively related to air quality improvement. Furthermore, the increase in economic development,

Clean and efficient use of coal in China entered the implementation stage in 2015, and a series of policies were established to promote the sustainable development of clean coal technologies (CCTs). However, the long-term effects of the clean coal technology policies (CCTPs) have not been evaluated, and the outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) would bring great uncertainty regarding the effectiveness evaluation. Employing deep learning approaches and spatial autocorrelation analysis, the present work intended to explore the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs, and investigate the association between air quality and socioeconomic factors to explore the internal mechanism of air quality improvement. Results showed that after implementing CCTPs, the air quality index (AQI) had an average reduction of  $18.82\%$  and the identical drop in air pollution in 2018 implied a 2-year time lag of CCTPs. Additionally, emission reduction and deindustrialization were explored as two promising ways to improve air quality while promoting energy transformation. The findings indicated that the Chinese government ought to pay more attention to long-term plans; industrial restructuring and environmental protection should be insisted upon to realize sustainable energy development. By providing a perspective on air quality improvement after the policies are implemented, this study can lead practitioners and academia to a comprehensive and objective view of clean coal policies.

Different from the previous policy effectiveness studies, which focused on a specific region or policy with little to national and industrial overview, the main goal of this study is to systematically and comprehensively measure the air quality variation after the implementation of CCTPs at the national level. Based on China's air quality and meteorological data from 2013 to 2019, deep learning approaches were applied to forecast the air quality in 2020, eliminating the uncertainties that COVID-19 brought about. Then, the spatial autocorrelation analysis was utilized to investigate the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of air pollution. And applying socioeconomic data, the association between AQI change and socioeconomic factors was calculated to explore the most likely mechanism of CCTPs. Our findings showed that there was a significant air quality improvement after the implementation of CCTPs, indicating that clean coal transformation adapts to the requirements of sustainable development in China. And two main channels to reduce air pollution were excavated, namely industrialization and environmental governance. This is essential to reaching an agreement on clean coal utilization, supporting the formulation of energy policies, and combating climate change.

related policies have reached a consensus. Nevertheless, the contributions mainly focus on regions with high-polluting and energy-intensive industries or target a single kind of clean coal policy. Energy transition is a systematic initiative that requires a long-term and holistic vision to guide nations, industries, companies, and individuals to collaborate from all aspects (Wei et al., 2022). The efficient and effective implementation of CCTPs also emphasizes the interrelatedness of social, technical, institutional and political changes (Markard, 2018), and sets the tone for atmospheric administration in a certain period. Therefore, this paper investigates the effectiveness of CCTPs at the national level with the industrial background of coal-based clean energy, and pays attention to how air quality varied after the implementation of CCTPs. Second, in the existing studies, the mechanism of energy transition policies on environmental pollution control is rarely discussed. By clarifying the association between the AQI and socioeconomic factors, this study tries to provide quantitative results for the internal mechanism of air quality improvement.

where  $C_{AQI}$  is the change rate of the AQI for each studied city,  $pr_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month for the studied cities from 2013 to 2015, and  $po_{AQI}$  is the average AQI of each month from 2016 to 2020. The calculated results vary between  $-100\%$  and  $+100\%$ , where negative and positive values indicate improvement or deterioration of air quality, and 0 represents no change.

To comprehensively explore the association between air quality change and socioeconomic factors, and reflect the internal mechanism of CCTPs working on air quality improvement, the correlation coefficients were calculated with all socioeconomic factors separately using Equation (13). Ordinary least squares regression was also carried out, and the variance inflation factor of each parameter was computed to verify the existence of multicollinearity.

To better understand the policy effect on air quality improvement, the change rate of AQI was computed between the pre- and post-policy nodes, as shown in Table 4. All the areas experienced improved air quality, with the change rate of AQI ranging from  $4.28\%$  to  $29.54\%$ . Regionally, the degree of improvement was maximum in central China  $(25.61\%)$ , followed by northeast  $(25.47\%)$ , eastern  $(20.88\%)$ , and northern China  $(18.30\%)$ . The three districts with the least improvement were northwest, southern, and southwest China, with change rates of  $15.27\%$ $14.63\%$  and  $14.44\%$  respectively. On average, the AQI value was reduced by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020, indicating that China's air quality was greatly improved after the implementation of CCTPs.

During the period of CCTPs implementation, an obvious air quality improvement was observed in the Chinese mainland, manifested as a decrease of AQI value by  $18.82\%$  from 2013 to 2020. It is concluded that the clean and efficient transformation of coal resources conforms to China's national conditions, and could indeed improve the atmospheric environment, which has been demonstrated in the existing research as well (Li and Patino-Echeverri, 2017; Liu et al., 2021). Specifically, correlation coefficients showed that the promotion of emission reduction and deindustrialization could fundamentally help to reduce airborne contamination, although their persistence relies on continued efforts and is influenced by various factors (Yang and Teng, 2018). Another important observation was a 2-year time lag before the stringent CCTPs starting from 2015 takes any strong positive effects, shown as a dramatical reduction of AQI in 2018. It implies that the influence of time lag should be considered in the decision-making process, as a time lag commonly appears in air pollution regulations and plans (Van der et al., 2017).

Limitations in both data acquisition and method selection should be acknowledged. First, the air quality variation before and after the implementation of CCTPs was investigated over a time span of 7 years, owing to the AQI data is available since 2013. The future study would be enriched with broader periods to observe a long-term performance. For method selection, the first limitation is that only CNN and LSTM were applied, ensemble-based approaches would be explored and incorporated along with the proposed model for further improvement in the prediction performance of the proposed framework. Further, the five socioeconomic factors were extracted and analyzed based on existing research and empirical knowledge. Future studies should continue to examine the deeper mechanisms of CCTPs using more quantitative and modelling methods.

metadata.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
references: In this study, such a test is conducted for one province in China as an example, Guizhou, where coal with extremely high sulfur content is used in power generation. As shown in Fig. 2, the  $95\%$  CIs using the sector-based method is largest for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ $(-30\% \sim 108\%)$  and smallest for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ $(-25\% \sim 20\%)$ . Compared to  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , the  $95\%$  CI of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission is relatively large, reaching  $-40\% \sim 42\%$ , and the variation of sulfur contents is estimated to contribute almost  $90\%$  to that uncertainty. Employing a unit-based method, the  $95\%$  CIs for all species are significantly reduced, with the largest ranging  $-9\% \sim 18\%$ , for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the smallest  $-8\% \sim 8\%$ , for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ . Figure 3 shows the difference in distributions of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions applying the two methods. The test confirms that the uncertainties of provincial and national emissions from power plants may be larger using a sector-based method compared to a unit-based one. Lacking detailed source information, however, such judgment cannot be easily extrapolated to other sectors without similar tests.
